does: This paper introduces SAEParate, a method for unlearning unwanted concepts in text-to-image diffusion models by improving sparse autoencoder (SAE) representations. The approach organizes latent features into concept-specific clusters using a concept-aware contrastive objective, allowing the model to suppress target concepts more precisely without modifying underlying parameters. The method enhances the SAE encoder with a GeLU-based nonlinear transformation to increase representation capacity and disentanglement, enabling more discriminative clustering of concepts. Experiments on the UnlearnCanvas benchmark demonstrate state-of-the-art unlearning performance with reduced interference between unrelated concepts.
connects: This work extends recent sparse autoencoder-based unlearning methods that leverage latent feature manipulation to suppress concepts without parameter updates, building on the efficiency advantages of these approaches over full model retraining. It directly addresses a limitation of prior SAE-based unlearning: the lack of explicit concept separation leading to shared latent features that cause unintended suppression of related concepts. The paper combines sparse reconstruction objectives with contrastive learning principles established in representation learning, applying disentanglement techniques from interpretability research to the unlearning domain. It responds to growing concerns about preventing harmful content generation in generative models while maintaining model utility.
enables: This work opens several directions for improving concept manipulation in generative models. The disentangled representation approach could enable more granular control over which specific aspects of concepts are suppressed while preserving related capabilities. The methodology may extend to unlearning in other generative architectures beyond diffusion models, including language models or multimodal systems. Future work could explore hierarchical concept organization for handling concept relationships more systematically, investigate how disentanglement quality affects downstream generation tasks, and develop methods for quantifying and predicting interference between concepts. The approach also suggests possibilities for selective unlearning that preserves beneficial aspects of concepts while removing harmful manifestations.
